glossary.def: Replacing a tabular value function or policy with a parameterized model — almost always a neural network — so that learning in one state generalizes to similar unseen states. It is what makes 'deep' RL possible on pixel or continuous state spaces, and the price of admission for the deadly triad's instability.

Sutton and Barto's name for the three ingredients that together risk divergence in value-based RL: function approximation, bootstrapping (a target that uses the network's own estimate), and off-policy learning. Each is benign alone and hard to give up; present together, value estimates can chase their own moving target without bound.

A circular buffer storing past transitions (s, a, r, s') from which DQN samples random minibatches for training. It decorrelates the highly similar consecutive frames that would otherwise violate the i.i.d. assumption SGD relies on, and lets each costly environment interaction drive many gradient updates.

A periodically-frozen copy of the value network whose parameters compute the regression target in the DQN loss, so the bootstrap target does not move while the online network is trained toward it. Refreshed by a hard copy every C steps or by a slow Polyak (exponential moving average) update θ⁻ ← τθ + (1−τ)θ⁻.

Proximal Policy Optimization's core loss: it multiplies the advantage by the new/old policy probability ratio r_t but clips r_t to [1−ε, 1+ε] and takes the pessimistic minimum, so once an update would move the policy too far in the rewarding direction the gradient switches off. A first-order, one-line approximation to TRPO's KL trust region; the default ε is 0.2.

An objective that adds a per-step entropy bonus αH(π) to the return, paying the agent to stay as random as it can while still performing well. It turns exploration into a first-class term rather than a bolt-on heuristic and underpins Soft Actor-Critic, the sample-efficient off-policy default for continuous control.

The phenomenon, documented by Henderson et al. (2018), that identical deep-RL code with identical hyperparameters can yield wildly different learning curves when only the random seed changes — because the agent generates its own data and tiny early differences compound. It is why credible deep-RL results report many seeds with their spread, not a single curve.

refs.cite: Mnih, V. et al. (2015). Human-level control through deep reinforcement learning. Nature 518.

Haarnoja, T., Zhou, A., Abbeel, P. & Levine, S. (2018). Soft Actor-Critic: Off-Policy Maximum Entropy Deep RL with a Stochastic Actor. arXiv:1801.01290.
van Hasselt, H., Guez, A. & Silver, D. (2016). Deep Reinforcement Learning with Double Q-learning. AAAI 2016 (arXiv:1509.06461).
Lillicrap, T. P. et al. (2016). Continuous control with deep reinforcement learning. arXiv:1509.02971.
Fujimoto, S., van Hoof, H. & Meger, D. (2018). Addressing Function Approximation Error in Actor-Critic Methods. arXiv:1802.09477.
Henderson, P. et al. (2018). Deep Reinforcement Learning that Matters. AAAI 2018 (arXiv:1709.06560).
